ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301716 covers waffle as a food product; chocolate filling variant is not separately coded — closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate waffle; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chocolate waffle weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a snack or light dessert.

nutrient_source.title: Waffle (chocolate) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Waffle (chocolate), CR ID F11100030. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for chocolate-filled waffle in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated using plain waffle (GI ~76, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) as the closest analogous food. The chocolate spread filling adds sugar but does not substantially alter the waffle matrix GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 41.35) / 100 ≈ 31.4, rounded to 31.3 for an 80g serving (41.35g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (24.2g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.1g/100g); sodium is low at 111mg/100g but excess sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chocolate waffle consumption due to high sugar (24.2g/100g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even though sodium is relatively low at 111mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (76), high sugar (24.2g/100g), high carbohydrate (51.7g/100g), and low dietary fibre (2.1g/100g) produce a very high glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chocolate waffles are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to a high glycemic index of 76, sugar content of 24.2g/100g exceeding the 20g/100g threshold, and a glycemic load of approximately 31 per 80g serving, which will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5g/100g) from butter and chocolate spread, combined with high sugar (24.2g/100g), raises LDL cholesterol and promotes atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid chocolate waffles due to high saturated fat (~8.5g/100g) and high sugar (24.2g/100g), both of which contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5g/100g) and high sugar (24.2g/100g) promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, outweighing any anti-inflammatory benefit from cocoa polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chocolate waffles as the high saturated fat and sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; substituting with unsweetened dark chocolate (>85% cocoa) in a lower-sugar preparation may reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate waffle is a sweet snack food made from a leavened pancake-style batter cooked in a waffle iron to produce a crisp, grid-patterned exterior with a soft, airy interior, filled or layered with chocolate spread. The batter typically contains wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, sugar, and a leavening agent, while the chocolate filling contributes additional sugar, fat, and cocoa solids. The result is a rich, sweet, slightly crispy snack with a tender crumb and a pronounced chocolate flavour. Per 100g, the food provides 397 kcal, 6.72g protein, 18.28g fat, 51.69g carbohydrate, 24.16g sugar, 2.13g dietary fibre, and 111mg sodium, as recorded in the Singapore HPB Foodo database. The high sugar content (24.2g/100g) and high glycemic index (~76) make this food clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Chocolate waffles are widely consumed as a street snack or café item in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often served warm. They are not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets and contain common allergens including gluten, eggs, milk, and soy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate waffle?
Is chocolate waffle high in sugar?
Is chocolate waffle suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of a chocolate waffle?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (CR ID F11100030) with confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain waffle, GI 76) in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, with analogous-estimate confidence. Saturated fat estimated at ~8.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable chocolate-filled waffle products, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chocolate waffle not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11100030) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 8.5g/100g using USDA FoodData Central entry for waffles with chocolate filling as the closest approved-source analogue; this value is marked as estimated. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this item. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on plain waffle (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chocolate-filled waffle was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 80g serving = (76 × 41.35) / 100 ≈ 31.3. The input record listed GI as 40 (low) from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is a forbidden/unsupported source and has been replaced with the approved analogous estimate of 76 from Atkinson et al. 2008. This food is not a Singaporean dish by origin; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names are required or provided.

Added saturated_fat_g (8.5, estimated) using USDA FoodData Central as approved secondary source.
Documented all -1 fields in notes with reason and source consulted.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with honest gap notes.
Expanded aliases to five English variants; verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.
Added all three required health_context conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with clinical specificity.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and nutritional context.
Confirmed no mandatory multilingual names required for Western/International cuisine origin.
